---
title: "A Multilingual Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990--2024: Construction, Validation, and Semantic Embeddings"
author:
  - name: "Minh Ha-Duong"
    affiliation:
      - name: "CNRS"
      - name: "CIRED"
date: "2026-03-20"
metadata-files: [data-paper-vars.yml]
bibliography: bibliography/main.bib
abstract: |
  We present a multilingual, deduplicated corpus of {{< meta corpus_total >}} works
  on climate finance published between 1990 and 2024, assembled from
  {{< meta corpus_sources >}} bibliographic sources: OpenAlex, Semantic Scholar,
  ISTEX, bibCNRS, SciSpace, and institutional grey literature. The search
  strategy targets eight languages through a four-tier keyword taxonomy.
  A six-flag quality pipeline combining metadata checks, citation isolation
  detection, semantic outlier filtering, and cross-encoder relevance scoring
  (human-validated AUC = 0.818) produces a curated dataset with full audit
  trail. Pre-computed 384-dimensional sentence-transformer embeddings
  (paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2) place all works in a shared
  semantic space regardless of language, enabling clustering, topic modelling,
  and temporal analysis without re-encoding. The corpus, embeddings, citation
  network, cluster assignments, and audit log are deposited in Zenodo under
  CC BY 4.0. All construction and validation scripts are publicly available.
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
---

<!-- Target journal: Scientific Data (Springer Nature) — Data Descriptor format -->
<!-- Constraints: abstract max 170 words (no refs, no findings), B&S max 700 words -->
<!-- Fallback: Data Science Journal (CODATA/Ubiquity Press) — Research Paper format -->
<!-- See docs/journal-survey-data-paper.md for full journal assessment -->

## Background & Summary

Climate finance---the financial flows directed at mitigating and adapting to climate change---has become one of the most politically salient objects in international economic governance. The \$100 billion annual commitment made at Copenhagen in 2009, and the \$300 billion New Collective Quantified Goal agreed at Baku in 2024, are contested not only in their adequacy but in their very definition: what counts as climate finance depends on accounting conventions that are themselves the subject of ongoing dispute [@roberts_weikmans2017; @michaelowa2007].

Recent bibliometric and scientometric work has begun to map the climate finance literature [@care_weber2023; @shang_jin2023; @nalimov_miskiewicz2023], but existing datasets are either narrowly scoped (single database, English only) or lack the curation needed for reproducible analysis. This data descriptor presents a multilingual, deduplicated, and validated corpus of {{< meta corpus_total >}} works on climate finance published between 1990 and 2024, assembled from {{< meta corpus_sources >}} bibliographic sources spanning academic publications, institutional grey literature, and curated teaching materials. The corpus is accompanied by {{< meta emb_dimensions >}}-dimensional sentence-transformer embeddings for all works with titles and publication years in the 1990--2024 range, enabling semantic analysis without requiring users to re-run the embedding pipeline.

The dataset was constructed to support the historical analysis presented in @haduong2026, but its scope and curation make it suitable for a wide range of research applications: topic modelling, citation network analysis, bibliometric mapping of the climate finance field, and cross-lingual studies of how climate finance is discussed in different linguistic traditions.

The corpus is multilingual by design. While English-language works dominate ({{< meta lang_english_pct >}}%), the search strategy explicitly targeted French (*finance climat*, *finance climatique*), Chinese (气候融资, 气候金融), Japanese (気候金融, グリーンファイナンス), and German-language literature through ISTEX, bibCNRS, and multilingual OpenAlex queries. This deliberate multilingual scope, though it yields a modest non-English share, provides a foundation for cross-linguistic studies that monolingual corpora cannot support.

Key statistics:

- **{{< meta corpus_total >}}** refined works after deduplication and quality filtering
- **{{< meta corpus_with_embeddings >}}** works with sentence-transformer embeddings ({{< meta emb_dimensions >}} dimensions)
- **{{< meta corpus_core >}}** core works (cited $\geq$ {{< meta corpus_core_threshold >}} times), forming the field's intellectual backbone
- **{{< meta corpus_sources >}} sources**: OpenAlex, Semantic Scholar, ISTEX, bibCNRS, SciSpace, grey literature (OECD/UNFCCC/World Bank/CPI)
- **Languages**: English ({{< meta lang_english_pct >}}%), Portuguese, Spanish, French, Chinese, Japanese, German, and others
- **Full audit trail**: every inclusion/exclusion decision recorded in `corpus_audit.csv`

## Methods

### Corpus construction

{{< include _includes/corpus-construction.md >}}

### Corpus refinement

{{< include _includes/corpus-refinement.md >}}

### Embedding generation

{{< include _includes/embedding-generation.md >}}

## Data Records

The dataset consists of the following files, deposited in the Zenodo repository and also reproducible from source via the pipeline scripts. Locally, files are stored in a configurable data directory (`CLIMATE_FINANCE_DATA` environment variable).

### refined_works.csv

The primary corpus file. Each row represents one deduplicated work.

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `id` | string | OpenAlex work ID (where available) or source-specific identifier |
| `doi` | string | Normalized DOI (lowercased, prefix stripped); may be empty |
| `title` | string | Work title |
| `author` | string | First author or "Unknown" |
| `year` | integer | Publication year (1990--2024) |
| `abstract` | string | Abstract text; empty for works without abstracts |
| `cited_by_count` | integer | Maximum citation count across all source records |
| `source` | string | Primary source (highest-priority source that contributed this record) |
| `source_count` | integer | Number of distinct sources contributing this record (sum of `from_*`) |
| `from_openalex` | integer (0/1) | Whether OpenAlex contributed this record |
| `from_semanticscholar` | integer (0/1) | Whether Semantic Scholar contributed this record |
| `from_istex` | integer (0/1) | Whether ISTEX contributed this record |
| `from_bibcnrs` | integer (0/1) | Whether bibCNRS contributed this record |
| `from_scispsace` | integer (0/1) | Whether SciSpace contributed this record |
| `from_grey` | integer (0/1) | Whether grey literature search contributed this record |
| `from_teaching` | integer (0/1) | Whether teaching canon contributed this record |
| `type` | string | Work type (article, book-chapter, report, etc.) |
| `language` | string | Detected language code (ISO 639-1) |
| `venue` | string | Publication venue (journal, series, or publisher) |
| `is_flagged` | boolean | Whether the work was flagged during refinement |
| `is_protected` | boolean | Whether the work was protected from removal |
| `flag_reason` | string | Reason for flagging (if applicable) |

**Size:** {{< meta corpus_total >}} rows.

### embeddings.npz

Compressed NumPy archive containing L2-normalized sentence-transformer embeddings. Each work's embedded text concatenates its title, abstract (if available), and keywords. Rows correspond to works in `refined_works.csv` with non-empty titles and publication years 1990--2024.

**Contents:** `vectors` (N $\times$ 384 float32), `keys` (DOI or source_id per row), `model` (model name), `text_fields` (input specification). The keyed cache supports incremental updates --- only new works are encoded when the corpus grows.

**Model:** `paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2` (sentence-transformers library).

### corpus_audit.csv

Complete audit trail recording every inclusion/exclusion decision during corpus refinement. Each row corresponds to one work from the pre-refinement `unified_works.csv`.

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `id` | string | Work identifier |
| `decision` | string | `kept`, `removed`, or `protected` |
| `flag_reason` | string | Why the work was flagged (if applicable) |
| `protection_reason` | string | Why the work was protected (if applicable) |

### semantic_clusters.csv

KMeans cluster assignments (k=6) for all embedded works.

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `id` | string | Work identifier |
| `cluster` | integer | Cluster label (0--5) |
| `umap_x` | float | UMAP 2D projection, x-coordinate |
| `umap_y` | float | UMAP 2D projection, y-coordinate |

### citations.csv

Internal citation network extracted via Crossref and OpenAlex enrichment.

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `cited_id` | string | Work identifier of the cited paper |
| `citing_id` | string | Work identifier of the citing paper |

### Source-specific catalog files

Individual source catalogs are preserved as `{source}_works.csv` files (e.g., `openalex_works.csv`, `istex_works.csv`), enabling provenance tracking back to individual sources. The merge script (`catalog_merge.py`) combines these into `unified_works.csv`, from which `refined_works.csv` is derived.

## Technical Validation

### Deduplication effectiveness

The merge pipeline reduces raw records through two passes:

1. **DOI-based deduplication** normalizes DOIs and merges records sharing the same DOI, retaining the maximum citation count and best metadata by source priority.
2. **Title+year deduplication** catches records lacking DOIs by matching on normalized title and publication year.

The `source_count` field tracks multi-source agreement: works appearing in 2+ independent sources have higher confidence. Of the {{< meta corpus_total >}} refined works, {{< meta corpus_multi_source >}} ({{< meta corpus_multi_source_pct >}}%) appear in multiple sources.

### Relevance validation

Relevance filtering uses a cross-encoder reranker model (`BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3`, 568M parameters) to score each work against the query "climate policy and financial mechanisms." The model was calibrated on weak labels (teaching syllabi, high-citation papers, multi-source overlap as positives; metadata-deficient and blacklist-matched works as negatives) achieving AUC = 0.766. Human validation on a stratified sample of 100 works yielded AUC = 0.818 and 81% accuracy (precision = 74%, recall = 76%).

An independent LLM audit (`google/gemma-2-27b-it` via OpenRouter) on a stratified random sample of 100 works (50 flagged, 50 unflagged) provides a second check. Type I error (flagged but LLM-relevant) and Type II error (unflagged but LLM-irrelevant) rates are reported in `corpus_audit.csv`. Both validation methods serve as independent checks on the rule-based flagging pipeline, not as ground truth, since relevance to "climate finance" is itself a contested category.

### Coverage analysis

The {{< meta corpus_sources >}}-source design provides complementary coverage (@tbl-sources). OpenAlex dominates in volume but the smaller sources contribute unique records absent from academic databases: bibCNRS adds 202 non-English works, grey literature provides 88 institutional reports, and the teaching canon contributes 16 pedagogical references. ISTEX's 4 records all survived refinement---3 were independently discovered by OpenAlex, confirming cross-source consistency. ISBN coverage is not reported because the corpus metadata does not include ISBNs; book-chapter identification relies on DOI and work-type fields instead.

{{< include _includes/tab_corpus_sources.md >}}

### Embedding quality

The multilingual sentence-transformer model (`paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2`) places abstracts in English, French, Chinese, Japanese, and German into a shared 384-dimensional semantic space. Embedding quality was validated through:

- **UMAP visualization**: 2D projection shows coherent thematic clusters with clear spatial separation between subfields (carbon markets, green finance, adaptation, renewable energy, forestry/REDD+, social/governance)
- **KMeans clustering** (k=6): produces interpretable thematic groups whose labels align with established subfield boundaries in the climate finance literature
- **Cross-lingual coherence**: non-English works cluster with thematically similar English-language works rather than forming language-specific clusters, confirming the multilingual model's ability to bridge linguistic boundaries
- **Nearest-neighbour spot checks**: for a random sample of 50 works, the 5 nearest neighbours by cosine similarity were manually assessed as thematically coherent in 92% of cases

## Usage Notes

### Reproducing the dataset

{{< include _includes/reproducibility.md >}}

### Reuse potential

The corpus and embeddings are designed to support multiple research directions beyond the companion study [@haduong2026]:

- **Topic modelling**: the pre-computed embeddings serve as input for BERTopic, top2vec, or other embedding-based topic models, avoiding the need to re-encode ~30,000 documents
- **Citation network analysis**: `citations.csv` enables co-citation, bibliographic coupling, and citation genealogy studies within the climate finance field
- **Cross-lingual comparison**: the multilingual embeddings place non-English works in the same semantic space as English ones, enabling cross-lingual clustering without translation
- **Temporal evolution**: the 34-year span (1990--2024) supports diachronic analyses of concept emergence, vocabulary change, and field structure dynamics
- **Semantic search and retrieval**: the L2-normalized embeddings support cosine-similarity-based search for finding related works
- **Field boundary studies**: the `source_count` and `from_*` provenance columns enable analysis of how different databases and discovery strategies define the boundaries of "climate finance" differently

### Limitations

- Non-English coverage, while deliberate, remains limited (8.6% of corpus). Studies requiring deep coverage of specific non-English traditions should supplement this corpus with targeted searches.
- Citation counts reflect OpenAlex data as of the collection date and are biased toward older and English-language works.
- The LLM audit provides a probabilistic check, not a definitive relevance judgment. Users with strict inclusion criteria should apply their own filtering.
- Grey literature records often lack abstracts, limiting their utility for embedding-based analyses.

## Code Availability

All scripts for corpus construction, refinement, and embedding generation are available in the project repository at [URL to be added upon acceptance]. The pipeline is organised into three phases:

- **Phase 1 (corpus building)**: `catalog_openalex.py`, `catalog_istex.py`, `catalog_grey.py`, `build_teaching_canon.py`, `catalog_merge.py`, `corpus_refine.py`, `enrich_embeddings.py` --- discovery, merge, enrichment, and quality filtering
- **Phase 2 (analysis)**: `analyze_embeddings.py` and downstream scripts --- UMAP projection, clustering, and derived analyses
- **Phase 3 (rendering)**: Quarto documents producing PDF and DOCX outputs

The pipeline requires Python $\geq$ 3.10 and is managed with `uv` (dependency resolution from `pyproject.toml`). DVC (Data Version Control) tracks the pipeline DAG and ensures reproducibility. A `Makefile` provides convenience targets: `make corpus` (Phase 1), `make figures` (Phase 2), `make manuscript` (Phase 3).

Scripts are organised in `scripts/` with a shared `utils.py` module providing data path resolution and common utilities.

## Data Availability

The dataset is deposited in Zenodo at [https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19097045](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19097045). The deposit includes `refined_works.csv`, `embeddings.npz`, `corpus_audit.csv`, `semantic_clusters.csv`, and `citations.csv`. The dataset is released under CC BY 4.0.

Two sources require institutional credentials for re-collection: bibCNRS (CNRS Janus login) and SciSpace (commercial tool). Their raw exports are included in the Zenodo deposit to ensure full reproducibility despite access restrictions. All other sources (OpenAlex, ISTEX, grey literature, teaching canon) are collected via public APIs and can be re-harvested from the repository scripts.
